ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for peanut paste dessert soup; mapped to broader peanut food product class as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for sweetened peanut paste dessert soup; A0CKP (peanuts, prepared/processed) is the closest defensible broader match.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for peanut paste dessert soup; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of peanut paste dessert soup is approximately 250 g, as typically served at Singaporean hawker centres and dessert stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Peanut paste — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Peanut paste (F02050013). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — peanuts and peanut-based products
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 14 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database value for peanuts (GI ~14, low). Peanut paste dessert soup is a ground peanut preparation with added sugar; the low GI of peanuts dominates due to high fat and protein content slowing glucose absorption. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (14 × 24.75) / 100 ≈ 3.5 for a 250 g bowl (24.75 g carbohydrate). This is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific preparation was found in the University of Sydney GI Database.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (6 mg/100 g); moderate sugar content (7.4 g/100 g) below the solid-food hard gate of 10 g/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Peanut paste dessert soup is suitable for individuals with hypertension given its very low sodium content of 6 mg/100 g; however, portion size should be moderated and reduced-sugar preparation is preferred to limit cardiovascular glycaemic load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar 7.4 g/100 g with low dietary fibre 1.3 g/100 g; despite a low GI of 14 driven by peanut fat and protein, the added sugar content warrants caution in regular consumption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume peanut paste dessert soup only occasionally and in small portions (≤150 g), requesting minimal added sugar preparation, as the 7.4 g/100 g sugar with only 1.3 g/100 g fibre provides insufficient fibre modulation of the glycaemic response despite the overall low GI.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Predominantly unsaturated fat from peanuts (total fat 6.61 g/100 g, estimated saturated fat ~1.1 g/100 g); low sodium; moderate added sugar
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Peanut paste dessert soup provides predominantly monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fats from peanuts which are cardioprotective; sodium is very low at 6 mg/100 g; however, added sugar of 7.4 g/100 g should be minimised by requesting reduced-sugar preparation to avoid adverse glycaemic and lipid effects in cardiovascular disease management.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar 7.4 g/100 g promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; insufficient anti-inflammatory factors (fibre only 1.3 g/100 g, no omega-3, no anti-inflammatory spices)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid the sweetened preparation of peanut paste; unsweetened peanut paste would be preferable as peanuts provide MUFA and vitamin E with anti-inflammatory potential, but the added sugar in this dessert formulation promotes inflammation and is not recommended for regular RA dietary management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Peanut paste dessert soup (花生糊, Hua Sheng Hu) is a smooth, thick, warm dessert soup widely consumed in Singapore and across Southern China. It is prepared by finely grinding roasted peanuts and cooking them with water and sugar until a creamy, porridge-like consistency is achieved. The texture is velvety and thick, with a rich, nutty sweetness derived from the peanuts and added sugar. It is commonly served warm in hawker centres, dessert stalls, and at home as a snack or dessert course. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 115 kcal, 3.9 g protein, 6.61 g fat (predominantly unsaturated from peanuts), 9.9 g carbohydrate, 7.4 g sugar, 1.3 g dietary fibre, and only 6 mg sodium. The glycemic index is low at approximately 14, driven by the high fat and protein content of peanuts which slow gastric emptying and glucose absorption. Despite the low GI, the added sugar content (7.4 g/100 g) with low fibre (1.3 g/100 g) makes this dessert unsuitable for frequent consumption in Type 2 Diabetes management. For hypertension, the very low sodium makes it generally suitable. For cardiovascular health, the predominantly unsaturated fat profile from peanuts is beneficial, though added sugar should be minimised. The dish is plant-based, gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal. It is a culturally significant comfort food in the Singaporean Chinese dessert tradition, often consumed as an evening snack or after a meal.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F02050013) with confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database value for peanuts (GI ~14), which is the primary ingredient; estimation basis is clearly documented. Multilingual names are well-established for this Singaporean Chinese dessert.
notes: Saturated fat estimated at 1.1 g/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central peanut composition data (approximately 16% of total fat is saturated for peanuts); HPB source does not report saturated fat breakdown separately. Potassium estimated at 120 mg/100 g based on USDA FoodData Central peanut soup analogue; HPB source does not report potassium. Cholesterol is 0.0 g confirmed as this is a plant-based food product with no animal-derived ingredients. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific peanut paste dessert soup preparation are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F02050013, and no exact match was found in USDA FoodData Central for this sweetened peanut paste soup formulation as of 2026. GI of 14 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database peanut entry; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific sweetened preparation. GL per serving = (14 × 9.9 g carbohydrate × 250 g serving / 100) / 100 = 3.5 for a 250 g bowl.
